link|Q17225914}} == Japanese content == {{Footnotes lacking|date=April 2024}} {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takaoshihime Shrine |Image = [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20241006 (1).jpg|270px]]
Torii gate |Location = {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} 387 Tokumaru |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|33|47|22.5|N|132|45|37|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Takaoshihime Ōkami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}; former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other names = Takaoshi-sama, Takaoshiho Myōjin |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival= October 13, 14, and 15 each year |Rituals = Dondoyaki, Mushiboshi Festival, Midō pilgrimage |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ehime|label=Takaoshihime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takaoshihime Shrine''' (たかおしひめじんじゃ, also written 高忍日売神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}, {{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}}. Its name appears in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} under {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}. It is the only shrine in Japan that enshrines Takaoshihime Ōkami and is revered as the ancestral deity of {{jalink|midwives|Q7832320}} and {{jalink|wet nurses|Q472898}}, attracting particular devotion from {{jalink|midwives|Q185196}} nationwide as well as people involved in education. Within the shrine grounds stands the “Monument to Mothers, Children, and Midwives,” erected by the {{jalink|Muramatsu Shihoko Midwife Memorial Association}}, and an event is held there every year on March 8. The shrine crests used are the “Sixteen-petal Chrysanthemum” and the “5–7 Paulownia.” The annual festival is held every year on October 13, 14, and 15. === Enshrined deities === ; Principal deity : {{jalink|Takaoshihime Ōkami}} ; Associated deities : {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q97613650}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}} === Mythology === Several unique myths have been handed down at this shrine. One concerns the birth of {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, the father of the first emperor {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}. When {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} were crossing the sea together in harmony, the goddess suddenly went into labor. They built a birthing hut on a nearby shore, and she gave birth there. They thatched the hut with ugaya (a term said by some to refer not to reeds but to a birthing hut itself)Some theories say this refers not to reeds but to a birthing hut., but many crabs crawled up from the sea and entered the hut, resulting in a very difficult labor. When {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} chanted the name of “Takaoshihime Ōkami” with single-minded devotion, Takaoshihime Ōkami appeared and dispatched {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q462555|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto}}. Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto and Ame no Oshio no Mikoto made brooms to sweep away the crabs, while Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto entered the hut and took on the role of midwife. As a result, although the birth was initially difficult, it became a safe delivery, and before the hut was fully completed, a healthy baby boy was born. {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} named the boy {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. From this myth, the shrine’s deity has been revered by many people as the ancestral deity of midwives and wet nurses, and also as a deity of brooms. === History === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251010 (3).jpg|thumb|Main sanctuary]] The date of the shrine’s founding is unknown. It is located in {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}} (which differs somewhat from the modern district). The area is bordered by mountains and hills to the south and plains to the north, blessed with abundant water and apparently developed early; excavations have uncovered large and small {{jalink|kofun groups|Q11411019}}, large-scale {{jalink|ritual sites}}, and settlement remains. From ancient times, the shrine enjoyed deep reverence from the imperial family, and it is said that imperial envoys were dispatched during childbirths. The {{jalink|Iyo no Kuni Fudoki fragments}} record that {{jalink|Prince Shōtoku|Q263972}} toured Iyo District during a visit to Dōgo and worshiped at this shrine, dedicating a “divine name plaque.” In the Nara period, the shrine became a developing landholder, cultivating the surrounding area and forming an extensive sacred precinct of over eight chō, known as “Three Thousand Bō in Front and One Thousand Bō Behind.” Some shrine priests concurrently served as provincial officials, maintaining active exchanges with the central government, and the shrine became a cultural hub of Iyo District. In the Heian period, the shrine was listed in volumes 9 and 10 of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} (compiled in 927), which records shrines supported by the state, and it also appears in the {{jalink|Iyo Province Jinmyōchō}}, indicating its importance. It was granted the divine rank of Senior Third Rank and was also called “Takaoshiho Myōjin,” with frequent visits by imperial envoys and provincial governors. In the Kamakura period, it was known as “Takaoshihime Wakamiya Hachiman-gū” and attracted the devotion of the warrior class; in particular, {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} donated 76 chō of shrine land for the prosperity of the shogunate and for safe childbirth. Markets (three-times-a-month markets) were held at Ebisu shrines located to the northeast (kimon) and southwest (ura-kimon) of the shrine, drawing large crowds. The shrine families formed warrior bands centered on the Ōkannushi and Ōmiya-guji and served as shogunal {{jalink|gokenin|Q63765}}, while also being valued by the imperial court. During the Nanboku-chō period, they supported the Southern Court, and {{jalink|Emperor Go-Murakami|Q440447}} dispatched an imperial envoy who granted rank and confirmed shrine lands to the then head priest Tō no Hyōe. The local lord Kawano (Doi) Michikage also donated shrine lands and arms. In the Muromachi period, the markets at the Ebisu shrines expanded to six times a month and flourished. The shrine acted as the seat authority, protecting merchants and engaging in trade not only within Iyo Province but nationwide. However, in the Sengoku period, as warlords pursued policies of enriching the country and strengthening the military, the shrine gradually lost its lands, merchants and artisans were gathered into castle towns, and the markets rapidly declined.This market continued to be licensed until the Kanbun era (1661–1673) of the Edo period to meet local demand. In the Edo period, successive Matsuyama domain lords of the Hisamatsu family, including {{jalink|Katō Yoshiaki|Q1371657}} and {{jalink|Gamō Tadatomo|Q8513824}}, showed deep reverence for the shrine. As the domain’s prayer site, rites were performed for safe childbirth, protection from calamity, abundant harvests, rain, and clear weather, and it served as the guardian shrine of the Matsuyama domain and the general tutelary shrine of Iyo District. A domain lord’s proxy office used for official visits still survives, and until the end of the shogunate there was a room known as the “Lord’s Audience Chamber.” It is said that, because it is the only shrine in Japan to enshrine Takaoshihime Ōkami, it was bestowed the highest divine rank of Senior First Rank and the title “Only Shrine in the Nation” by the imperial court. The ox beam used in the tenshu of {{jalink|Matsuyama Castle|Q981357}}, raised in 1850, is said to have come from a great pine tree that stood at the northwest corner of the shrine precincts and was visible even from {{jalink|Tōon City|Q132412}}. An ancient well within the grounds never dries up even during droughts, and drinking its spring water is believed to bring fertility, making it an object of faith among women. At the birth of {{jalink|Princess Kiyomiya Takako}}, an imperial midwife requested prayers for a safe delivery, which were conducted by the shrine’s priests. === Main annual events === ; January 1 : New Year’s Day Festival ; January 3 : Daily Offering Association Opening Festival ; January 15 : Dondoyaki ritual ; February 3 : Setsubun Festival (Star Deity Festival) ; February 11 : Kinensai ; March 8 : Safe Childbirth and Good Fortune Grand Festival (Day of Mothers, Children, and Midwives) ; April 29 : Spring Grand Festival ; Second Sunday of May : Shōshikai ; August 2 : Mushiboshi Festival ; October 13–15 : Grand Annual Festival ; November 23 : Niiname-sai ; December 31 : Great Purification Rite and Year-End Festival === Shrine grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20250420 (2).jpg|thumb|Worship hall]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251012 (8).jpg|thumb|160px|Entwined branches]] ; Worship hall : Many rare votive tablets, including “Four Seasons of Agriculture” paintings, are displayed. ; Middle hall : Includes the “Domain Lord’s Proxy Office” and the offering preparation hall. ; Connecting hall (Narrow hall) ; Main sanctuary : A three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof; periodic rebuilding was formerly carried out. ; Cloister ; Portable shrine storehouse ; Amulet office ; Purification pavilion ; Shrine gate ; Great torii === Sub-shrines within the grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (7).jpg|thumb|Ubusuna Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (1).jpg|thumb|Soga Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251010 (5).jpg|thumb|Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine]] ; Ubusuna Shrine : Affectionately called “Ubusuna-sama.” Taking home a round stone called the “Enman Stone” is believed to grant wishes; returning it in double quantity after the wish is fulfilled is said to bring even greater happiness. ; Soga Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Ōaza, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. Enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q313119}}. ; Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}. Formerly located outside the grounds as the guardian of the shrine’s northeast demon gate. ; Kotohira Shrine : Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto and Emperor Sutoku. ; Tokumaru Tenman Shrine ; Ikume Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} and {{jalink|Fujiwara no Kagekiyo|Q7676361}}. ; Amaterasu Kōtaijingū ; Narahara Shrine : Enshrines Uke Mochi-no-Kami. ; Shimoichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}; formerly guarded the shrine’s southwest demon gate. ; Ryūō Shrine : Formerly enshrined on an islet in a sacred pond; enshrines the Eight Great Dragon Kings. ; Funakawa Shrine (Funakawa Daimyōjin) ; Nanase Inari Shrine ; Suwa Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Kawara-gumi, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. ; Dōso Shrine ; Kasuga Shrine ; Tamahōki Shrine ; Ichinomiya Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Amemurakumo-no-Mikoto}}. ; Fukuga Shrine === Shrines outside the grounds === ; Soga Shrine : Located in Nakagawara Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Soga Shrine : Located in Ōma Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Hibuse Daimyōjin Shrine : Located within the Ōma Soga Shrine grounds; in the Edo period, magistrates stayed in seclusion there to pray for fire prevention and distributed talismans throughout the district. ; Narahara Shrine ; Sae Shrine ; Gotō Shrine (Gotō Daimyōjin) : Enshrines the ancestors of the Gotō family, a shrine family that maintained independence as jitō priests of Takaoshihime Shrine lands and served as the leading shrine priests of the Matsuyama domain. Until the late Edo period, they obtained official posts through the Hirohashi family and other court intermediaries without coming under the Yoshida family’s control. : The Gotō Shrine was enshrined near Miyashita in present-day {{jalink|Iyo City|Q838678}} until the mid-Edo period, but was relocated to its current site due to domain boundary issues. Image:Soga-jinja (Nakagawara, Masaki) 20241012 (1).jpg|Soga Shrine (Nakagawara) Image:Soga-jinja 20240630.jpg|Soga Shrine (Ōma) === On-site information === ; Address * {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} Tokumaru 387 ; Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Shikoku Railway Company|Q496531|JR}} {{jalink|Yosan Line|Q575016}} {{jalink|Kita-Iyo Station|Q898935}} (15 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * Masaki Town History Editorial Committee (ed.), *Masaki Town History*, 1979, Masaki Town Office * {{jalink|Nakarai Goan|Q121271640|Nakarai Goan}}, *Echoes of Ehime* (Iyo Historical Talks Society Series, Vol. 1), 1980, Iyo Historical Talks Society, Ehime Educational Books * Shikinaisha Research Association (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report*, Vol. 23, 1987, Kōgakkan University Press * Shiga Tsuyoshi, *Research on Shikinaisha*, Vol. 1, 1977, Yūzankaku Publishing === External links === {{Commonscat|Takaoshihime-jinja}} * [http://www.takaoshihime.jp/ Official website of Takaoshihime Shrine] * [http://ehime-jinjacyo.jp/?p=5034 Takaoshihime Shrine (Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association)] * [https://www.facebook.com/pages/%E9%AB%98%E5%BF%8D%E6%97%A5%E8%B3%A3%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/556783461036023 Official Facebook page of Takaoshihime Shrine] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takaoshihime Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Iyo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛媛県の神社]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の歴史]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の建築物]] [[Category:伊予国の式内社]]

Torii gate |Location = {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} 387 Tokumaru |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|33|47|22.5|N|132|45|37|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Takaoshihime Ōkami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}; former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other names = Takaoshi-sama, Takaoshiho Myōjin |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival= October 13, 14, and 15 each year |Rituals = Dondoyaki, Mushiboshi Festival, Midō pilgrimage |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ehime|label=Takaoshihime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takaoshihime Shrine''' (たかおしひめじんじゃ, also written 高忍日売神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}, {{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}}. Its name appears in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} under {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}. It is the only shrine in Japan that enshrines Takaoshihime Ōkami and is revered as the ancestral deity of {{jalink|midwives|Q7832320}} and {{jalink|wet nurses|Q472898}}, attracting particular devotion from {{jalink|midwives|Q185196}} nationwide as well as people involved in education. Within the shrine grounds stands the “Monument to Mothers, Children, and Midwives,” erected by the {{jalink|Muramatsu Shihoko Midwife Memorial Association}}, and an event is held there every year on March 8. The shrine crests used are the “Sixteen-petal Chrysanthemum” and the “5–7 Paulownia.” The annual festival is held every year on October 13, 14, and 15. === Enshrined deities === ; Principal deity : {{jalink|Takaoshihime Ōkami}} ; Associated deities : {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q97613650}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}} === Mythology === Several unique myths have been handed down at this shrine. One concerns the birth of {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, the father of the first emperor {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}. When {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} were crossing the sea together in harmony, the goddess suddenly went into labor. They built a birthing hut on a nearby shore, and she gave birth there. They thatched the hut with ugaya (a term said by some to refer not to reeds but to a birthing hut itself)Some theories say this refers not to reeds but to a birthing hut., but many crabs crawled up from the sea and entered the hut, resulting in a very difficult labor. When {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} chanted the name of “Takaoshihime Ōkami” with single-minded devotion, Takaoshihime Ōkami appeared and dispatched {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q462555|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto}}. Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto and Ame no Oshio no Mikoto made brooms to sweep away the crabs, while Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto entered the hut and took on the role of midwife. As a result, although the birth was initially difficult, it became a safe delivery, and before the hut was fully completed, a healthy baby boy was born. {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} named the boy {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. From this myth, the shrine’s deity has been revered by many people as the ancestral deity of midwives and wet nurses, and also as a deity of brooms. === History === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251010 (3).jpg|thumb|Main sanctuary]] The date of the shrine’s founding is unknown. It is located in {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}} (which differs somewhat from the modern district). The area is bordered by mountains and hills to the south and plains to the north, blessed with abundant water and apparently developed early; excavations have uncovered large and small {{jalink|kofun groups|Q11411019}}, large-scale {{jalink|ritual sites}}, and settlement remains. From ancient times, the shrine enjoyed deep reverence from the imperial family, and it is said that imperial envoys were dispatched during childbirths. The {{jalink|Iyo no Kuni Fudoki fragments}} record that {{jalink|Prince Shōtoku|Q263972}} toured Iyo District during a visit to Dōgo and worshiped at this shrine, dedicating a “divine name plaque.” In the Nara period, the shrine became a developing landholder, cultivating the surrounding area and forming an extensive sacred precinct of over eight chō, known as “Three Thousand Bō in Front and One Thousand Bō Behind.” Some shrine priests concurrently served as provincial officials, maintaining active exchanges with the central government, and the shrine became a cultural hub of Iyo District. In the Heian period, the shrine was listed in volumes 9 and 10 of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} (compiled in 927), which records shrines supported by the state, and it also appears in the {{jalink|Iyo Province Jinmyōchō}}, indicating its importance. It was granted the divine rank of Senior Third Rank and was also called “Takaoshiho Myōjin,” with frequent visits by imperial envoys and provincial governors. In the Kamakura period, it was known as “Takaoshihime Wakamiya Hachiman-gū” and attracted the devotion of the warrior class; in particular, {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} donated 76 chō of shrine land for the prosperity of the shogunate and for safe childbirth. Markets (three-times-a-month markets) were held at Ebisu shrines located to the northeast (kimon) and southwest (ura-kimon) of the shrine, drawing large crowds. The shrine families formed warrior bands centered on the Ōkannushi and Ōmiya-guji and served as shogunal {{jalink|gokenin|Q63765}}, while also being valued by the imperial court. During the Nanboku-chō period, they supported the Southern Court, and {{jalink|Emperor Go-Murakami|Q440447}} dispatched an imperial envoy who granted rank and confirmed shrine lands to the then head priest Tō no Hyōe. The local lord Kawano (Doi) Michikage also donated shrine lands and arms. In the Muromachi period, the markets at the Ebisu shrines expanded to six times a month and flourished. The shrine acted as the seat authority, protecting merchants and engaging in trade not only within Iyo Province but nationwide. However, in the Sengoku period, as warlords pursued policies of enriching the country and strengthening the military, the shrine gradually lost its lands, merchants and artisans were gathered into castle towns, and the markets rapidly declined.This market continued to be licensed until the Kanbun era (1661–1673) of the Edo period to meet local demand. In the Edo period, successive Matsuyama domain lords of the Hisamatsu family, including {{jalink|Katō Yoshiaki|Q1371657}} and {{jalink|Gamō Tadatomo|Q8513824}}, showed deep reverence for the shrine. As the domain’s prayer site, rites were performed for safe childbirth, protection from calamity, abundant harvests, rain, and clear weather, and it served as the guardian shrine of the Matsuyama domain and the general tutelary shrine of Iyo District. A domain lord’s proxy office used for official visits still survives, and until the end of the shogunate there was a room known as the “Lord’s Audience Chamber.” It is said that, because it is the only shrine in Japan to enshrine Takaoshihime Ōkami, it was bestowed the highest divine rank of Senior First Rank and the title “Only Shrine in the Nation” by the imperial court. The ox beam used in the tenshu of {{jalink|Matsuyama Castle|Q981357}}, raised in 1850, is said to have come from a great pine tree that stood at the northwest corner of the shrine precincts and was visible even from {{jalink|Tōon City|Q132412}}. An ancient well within the grounds never dries up even during droughts, and drinking its spring water is believed to bring fertility, making it an object of faith among women. At the birth of {{jalink|Princess Kiyomiya Takako}}, an imperial midwife requested prayers for a safe delivery, which were conducted by the shrine’s priests. === Main annual events === ; January 1 : New Year’s Day Festival ; January 3 : Daily Offering Association Opening Festival ; January 15 : Dondoyaki ritual ; February 3 : Setsubun Festival (Star Deity Festival) ; February 11 : Kinensai ; March 8 : Safe Childbirth and Good Fortune Grand Festival (Day of Mothers, Children, and Midwives) ; April 29 : Spring Grand Festival ; Second Sunday of May : Shōshikai ; August 2 : Mushiboshi Festival ; October 13–15 : Grand Annual Festival ; November 23 : Niiname-sai ; December 31 : Great Purification Rite and Year-End Festival === Shrine grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20250420 (2).jpg|thumb|Worship hall]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251012 (8).jpg|thumb|160px|Entwined branches]] ; Worship hall : Many rare votive tablets, including “Four Seasons of Agriculture” paintings, are displayed. ; Middle hall : Includes the “Domain Lord’s Proxy Office” and the offering preparation hall. ; Connecting hall (Narrow hall) ; Main sanctuary : A three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof; periodic rebuilding was formerly carried out. ; Cloister ; Portable shrine storehouse ; Amulet office ; Purification pavilion ; Shrine gate ; Great torii === Sub-shrines within the grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (7).jpg|thumb|Ubusuna Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (1).jpg|thumb|Soga Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251010 (5).jpg|thumb|Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine]] ; Ubusuna Shrine : Affectionately called “Ubusuna-sama.” Taking home a round stone called the “Enman Stone” is believed to grant wishes; returning it in double quantity after the wish is fulfilled is said to bring even greater happiness. ; Soga Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Ōaza, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. Enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q313119}}. ; Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}. Formerly located outside the grounds as the guardian of the shrine’s northeast demon gate. ; Kotohira Shrine : Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto and Emperor Sutoku. ; Tokumaru Tenman Shrine ; Ikume Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} and {{jalink|Fujiwara no Kagekiyo|Q7676361}}. ; Amaterasu Kōtaijingū ; Narahara Shrine : Enshrines Uke Mochi-no-Kami. ; Shimoichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}; formerly guarded the shrine’s southwest demon gate. ; Ryūō Shrine : Formerly enshrined on an islet in a sacred pond; enshrines the Eight Great Dragon Kings. ; Funakawa Shrine (Funakawa Daimyōjin) ; Nanase Inari Shrine ; Suwa Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Kawara-gumi, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. ; Dōso Shrine ; Kasuga Shrine ; Tamahōki Shrine ; Ichinomiya Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Amemurakumo-no-Mikoto}}. ; Fukuga Shrine === Shrines outside the grounds === ; Soga Shrine : Located in Nakagawara Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Soga Shrine : Located in Ōma Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Hibuse Daimyōjin Shrine : Located within the Ōma Soga Shrine grounds; in the Edo period, magistrates stayed in seclusion there to pray for fire prevention and distributed talismans throughout the district. ; Narahara Shrine ; Sae Shrine ; Gotō Shrine (Gotō Daimyōjin) : Enshrines the ancestors of the Gotō family, a shrine family that maintained independence as jitō priests of Takaoshihime Shrine lands and served as the leading shrine priests of the Matsuyama domain. Until the late Edo period, they obtained official posts through the Hirohashi family and other court intermediaries without coming under the Yoshida family’s control. : The Gotō Shrine was enshrined near Miyashita in present-day {{jalink|Iyo City|Q838678}} until the mid-Edo period, but was relocated to its current site due to domain boundary issues. Image:Soga-jinja (Nakagawara, Masaki) 20241012 (1).jpg|Soga Shrine (Nakagawara) Image:Soga-jinja 20240630.jpg|Soga Shrine (Ōma) === On-site information === ; Address * {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} Tokumaru 387 ; Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Shikoku Railway Company|Q496531|JR}} {{jalink|Yosan Line|Q575016}} {{jalink|Kita-Iyo Station|Q898935}} (15 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * Masaki Town History Editorial Committee (ed.), *Masaki Town History*, 1979, Masaki Town Office * {{jalink|Nakarai Goan|Q121271640|Nakarai Goan}}, *Echoes of Ehime* (Iyo Historical Talks Society Series, Vol. 1), 1980, Iyo Historical Talks Society, Ehime Educational Books * Shikinaisha Research Association (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report*, Vol. 23, 1987, Kōgakkan University Press * Shiga Tsuyoshi, *Research on Shikinaisha*, Vol. 1, 1977, Yūzankaku Publishing === External links === {{Commonscat|Takaoshihime-jinja}} * [http://www.takaoshihime.jp/ Official website of Takaoshihime Shrine] * [http://ehime-jinjacyo.jp/?p=5034 Takaoshihime Shrine (Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association)] * [https://www.facebook.com/pages/%E9%AB%98%E5%BF%8D%E6%97%A5%E8%B3%A3%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/556783461036023 Official Facebook page of Takaoshihime Shrine] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takaoshihime Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takaoshihime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Iyo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛媛県の神社]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の歴史]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の建築物]] [[Category:伊予国の式内社]]

link|Q17225914}} == Japanese content == {{Footnotes lacking|date=April 2024}} {{Shinto shrine |Name = Takaoshihime Shrine |Image = [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20241006 (1).jpg|270px]]
Torii gate |Location = {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} 387 Tokumaru |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|33|47|22.5|N|132|45|37|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Takaoshihime Ōkami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{jalink|Shikinaisha}}; former {{jalink|Gōsha|Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = Three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} |Other names = Takaoshi-sama, Takaoshiho Myōjin |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival= October 13, 14, and 15 each year |Rituals = Dondoyaki, Mushiboshi Festival, Midō pilgrimage |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ehime|label=Takaoshihime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takaoshihime Shrine''' (たかおしひめじんじゃ, also written 高忍日売神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}, {{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}}. Its name appears in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} under {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}. It is the only shrine in Japan that enshrines Takaoshihime Ōkami and is revered as the ancestral deity of {{jalink|midwives|Q7832320}} and {{jalink|wet nurses|Q472898}}, attracting particular devotion from {{jalink|midwives|Q185196}} nationwide as well as people involved in education. Within the shrine grounds stands the “Monument to Mothers, Children, and Midwives,” erected by the {{jalink|Muramatsu Shihoko Midwife Memorial Association}}, and an event is held there every year on March 8. The shrine crests used are the “Sixteen-petal Chrysanthemum” and the “5–7 Paulownia.” The annual festival is held every year on October 13, 14, and 15. === Enshrined deities === ; Principal deity : {{jalink|Takaoshihime Ōkami}} ; Associated deities : {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q97613650}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}} === Mythology === Several unique myths have been handed down at this shrine. One concerns the birth of {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, the father of the first emperor {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}. When {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} were crossing the sea together in harmony, the goddess suddenly went into labor. They built a birthing hut on a nearby shore, and she gave birth there. They thatched the hut with ugaya (a term said by some to refer not to reeds but to a birthing hut itself)Some theories say this refers not to reeds but to a birthing hut., but many crabs crawled up from the sea and entered the hut, resulting in a very difficult labor. When {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} chanted the name of “Takaoshihime Ōkami” with single-minded devotion, Takaoshihime Ōkami appeared and dispatched {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q462555|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto}}. Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto and Ame no Oshio no Mikoto made brooms to sweep away the crabs, while Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto entered the hut and took on the role of midwife. As a result, although the birth was initially difficult, it became a safe delivery, and before the hut was fully completed, a healthy baby boy was born. {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} named the boy {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. From this myth, the shrine’s deity has been revered by many people as the ancestral deity of midwives and wet nurses, and also as a deity of brooms. === History === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251010 (3).jpg|thumb|Main sanctuary]] The date of the shrine’s founding is unknown. It is located in {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}} (which differs somewhat from the modern district). The area is bordered by mountains and hills to the south and plains to the north, blessed with abundant water and apparently developed early; excavations have uncovered large and small {{jalink|kofun groups|Q11411019}}, large-scale {{jalink|ritual sites}}, and settlement remains. From ancient times, the shrine enjoyed deep reverence from the imperial family, and it is said that imperial envoys were dispatched during childbirths. The {{jalink|Iyo no Kuni Fudoki fragments}} record that {{jalink|Prince Shōtoku|Q263972}} toured Iyo District during a visit to Dōgo and worshiped at this shrine, dedicating a “divine name plaque.” In the Nara period, the shrine became a developing landholder, cultivating the surrounding area and forming an extensive sacred precinct of over eight chō, known as “Three Thousand Bō in Front and One Thousand Bō Behind.” Some shrine priests concurrently served as provincial officials, maintaining active exchanges with the central government, and the shrine became a cultural hub of Iyo District. In the Heian period, the shrine was listed in volumes 9 and 10 of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} (compiled in 927), which records shrines supported by the state, and it also appears in the {{jalink|Iyo Province Jinmyōchō}}, indicating its importance. It was granted the divine rank of Senior Third Rank and was also called “Takaoshiho Myōjin,” with frequent visits by imperial envoys and provincial governors. In the Kamakura period, it was known as “Takaoshihime Wakamiya Hachiman-gū” and attracted the devotion of the warrior class; in particular, {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} donated 76 chō of shrine land for the prosperity of the shogunate and for safe childbirth. Markets (three-times-a-month markets) were held at Ebisu shrines located to the northeast (kimon) and southwest (ura-kimon) of the shrine, drawing large crowds. The shrine families formed warrior bands centered on the Ōkannushi and Ōmiya-guji and served as shogunal {{jalink|gokenin|Q63765}}, while also being valued by the imperial court. During the Nanboku-chō period, they supported the Southern Court, and {{jalink|Emperor Go-Murakami|Q440447}} dispatched an imperial envoy who granted rank and confirmed shrine lands to the then head priest Tō no Hyōe. The local lord Kawano (Doi) Michikage also donated shrine lands and arms. In the Muromachi period, the markets at the Ebisu shrines expanded to six times a month and flourished. The shrine acted as the seat authority, protecting merchants and engaging in trade not only within Iyo Province but nationwide. However, in the Sengoku period, as warlords pursued policies of enriching the country and strengthening the military, the shrine gradually lost its lands, merchants and artisans were gathered into castle towns, and the markets rapidly declined.This market continued to be licensed until the Kanbun era (1661–1673) of the Edo period to meet local demand. In the Edo period, successive Matsuyama domain lords of the Hisamatsu family, including {{jalink|Katō Yoshiaki|Q1371657}} and {{jalink|Gamō Tadatomo|Q8513824}}, showed deep reverence for the shrine. As the domain’s prayer site, rites were performed for safe childbirth, protection from calamity, abundant harvests, rain, and clear weather, and it served as the guardian shrine of the Matsuyama domain and the general tutelary shrine of Iyo District. A domain lord’s proxy office used for official visits still survives, and until the end of the shogunate there was a room known as the “Lord’s Audience Chamber.” It is said that, because it is the only shrine in Japan to enshrine Takaoshihime Ōkami, it was bestowed the highest divine rank of Senior First Rank and the title “Only Shrine in the Nation” by the imperial court. The ox beam used in the tenshu of {{jalink|Matsuyama Castle|Q981357}}, raised in 1850, is said to have come from a great pine tree that stood at the northwest corner of the shrine precincts and was visible even from {{jalink|Tōon City|Q132412}}. An ancient well within the grounds never dries up even during droughts, and drinking its spring water is believed to bring fertility, making it an object of faith among women. At the birth of {{jalink|Princess Kiyomiya Takako}}, an imperial midwife requested prayers for a safe delivery, which were conducted by the shrine’s priests. === Main annual events === ; January 1 : New Year’s Day Festival ; January 3 : Daily Offering Association Opening Festival ; January 15 : Dondoyaki ritual ; February 3 : Setsubun Festival (Star Deity Festival) ; February 11 : Kinensai ; March 8 : Safe Childbirth and Good Fortune Grand Festival (Day of Mothers, Children, and Midwives) ; April 29 : Spring Grand Festival ; Second Sunday of May : Shōshikai ; August 2 : Mushiboshi Festival ; October 13–15 : Grand Annual Festival ; November 23 : Niiname-sai ; December 31 : Great Purification Rite and Year-End Festival === Shrine grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20250420 (2).jpg|thumb|Worship hall]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251012 (8).jpg|thumb|160px|Entwined branches]] ; Worship hall : Many rare votive tablets, including “Four Seasons of Agriculture” paintings, are displayed. ; Middle hall : Includes the “Domain Lord’s Proxy Office” and the offering preparation hall. ; Connecting hall (Narrow hall) ; Main sanctuary : A three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof; periodic rebuilding was formerly carried out. ; Cloister ; Portable shrine storehouse ; Amulet office ; Purification pavilion ; Shrine gate ; Great torii === Sub-shrines within the grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (7).jpg|thumb|Ubusuna Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (1).jpg|thumb|Soga Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251010 (5).jpg|thumb|Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine]] ; Ubusuna Shrine : Affectionately called “Ubusuna-sama.” Taking home a round stone called the “Enman Stone” is believed to grant wishes; returning it in double quantity after the wish is fulfilled is said to bring even greater happiness. ; Soga Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Ōaza, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. Enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q313119}}. ; Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}. Formerly located outside the grounds as the guardian of the shrine’s northeast demon gate. ; Kotohira Shrine : Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto and Emperor Sutoku. ; Tokumaru Tenman Shrine ; Ikume Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} and {{jalink|Fujiwara no Kagekiyo|Q7676361}}. ; Amaterasu Kōtaijingū ; Narahara Shrine : Enshrines Uke Mochi-no-Kami. ; Shimoichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}; formerly guarded the shrine’s southwest demon gate. ; Ryūō Shrine : Formerly enshrined on an islet in a sacred pond; enshrines the Eight Great Dragon Kings. ; Funakawa Shrine (Funakawa Daimyōjin) ; Nanase Inari Shrine ; Suwa Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Kawara-gumi, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. ; Dōso Shrine ; Kasuga Shrine ; Tamahōki Shrine ; Ichinomiya Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Amemurakumo-no-Mikoto}}. ; Fukuga Shrine === Shrines outside the grounds === ; Soga Shrine : Located in Nakagawara Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Soga Shrine : Located in Ōma Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Hibuse Daimyōjin Shrine : Located within the Ōma Soga Shrine grounds; in the Edo period, magistrates stayed in seclusion there to pray for fire prevention and distributed talismans throughout the district. ; Narahara Shrine ; Sae Shrine ; Gotō Shrine (Gotō Daimyōjin) : Enshrines the ancestors of the Gotō family, a shrine family that maintained independence as jitō priests of Takaoshihime Shrine lands and served as the leading shrine priests of the Matsuyama domain. Until the late Edo period, they obtained official posts through the Hirohashi family and other court intermediaries without coming under the Yoshida family’s control. : The Gotō Shrine was enshrined near Miyashita in present-day {{jalink|Iyo City|Q838678}} until the mid-Edo period, but was relocated to its current site due to domain boundary issues. Image:Soga-jinja (Nakagawara, Masaki) 20241012 (1).jpg|Soga Shrine (Nakagawara) Image:Soga-jinja 20240630.jpg|Soga Shrine (Ōma) === On-site information === ; Address * {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} Tokumaru 387 ; Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Shikoku Railway Company|Q496531|JR}} {{jalink|Yosan Line|Q575016}} {{jalink|Kita-Iyo Station|Q898935}} (15 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * Masaki Town History Editorial Committee (ed.), *Masaki Town History*, 1979, Masaki Town Office * {{jalink|Nakarai Goan|Q121271640|Nakarai Goan}}, *Echoes of Ehime* (Iyo Historical Talks Society Series, Vol. 1), 1980, Iyo Historical Talks Society, Ehime Educational Books * Shikinaisha Research Association (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report*, Vol. 23, 1987, Kōgakkan University Press * Shiga Tsuyoshi, *Research on Shikinaisha*, Vol. 1, 1977, Yūzankaku Publishing === External links === {{Commonscat|Takaoshihime-jinja}} * [http://www.takaoshihime.jp/ Official website of Takaoshihime Shrine] * [http://ehime-jinjacyo.jp/?p=5034 Takaoshihime Shrine (Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association)] * [https://www.facebook.com/pages/%E9%AB%98%E5%BF%8D%E6%97%A5%E8%B3%A3%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/556783461036023 Official Facebook page of Takaoshihime Shrine] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takaoshihime Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takaoshihime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takaoshihime Shrine]][[Category:Pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Iyo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛媛県の神社]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の歴史]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の建築物]] [[Category:伊予国の式内社]]

Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takaoshihime Shrine''' (たかおしひめじんじゃ, also written 高忍日売神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}, {{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}}. Its name appears in the {{jalink|Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932}} under {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}. It is the only shrine in Japan that enshrines Takaoshihime Ōkami and is revered as the ancestral deity of {{jalink|midwives|Q7832320}} and {{jalink|wet nurses|Q472898}}, attracting particular devotion from {{jalink|midwives|Q185196}} nationwide as well as people involved in education. Within the shrine grounds stands the “Monument to Mothers, Children, and Midwives,” erected by the {{jalink|Muramatsu Shihoko Midwife Memorial Association}}, and an event is held there every year on March 8. The shrine crests used are the “Sixteen-petal Chrysanthemum” and the “5–7 Paulownia.” The annual festival is held every year on October 13, 14, and 15. === Enshrined deities === ; Principal deity : {{jalink|Takaoshihime Ōkami}} ; Associated deities : {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q97613650}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}} : {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}} === Mythology === Several unique myths have been handed down at this shrine. One concerns the birth of {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, the father of the first emperor {{jalink|Emperor Jimmu|Q200188}}. When {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} and {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} were crossing the sea together in harmony, the goddess suddenly went into labor. They built a birthing hut on a nearby shore, and she gave birth there. They thatched the hut with ugaya (a term said by some to refer not to reeds but to a birthing hut itself)Some theories say this refers not to reeds but to a birthing hut., but many crabs crawled up from the sea and entered the hut, resulting in a very difficult labor. When {{jalink|Toyotama-hime no Mikoto}} chanted the name of “Takaoshihime Ōkami” with single-minded devotion, Takaoshihime Ōkami appeared and dispatched {{jalink|Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q462555|Ame no Oshio no Mikoto}}. Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto and Ame no Oshio no Mikoto made brooms to sweep away the crabs, while Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto entered the hut and took on the role of midwife. As a result, although the birth was initially difficult, it became a safe delivery, and before the hut was fully completed, a healthy baby boy was born. {{jalink|Hikohohodemi no Mikoto}} named the boy {{jalink|Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. From this myth, the shrine’s deity has been revered by many people as the ancestral deity of midwives and wet nurses, and also as a deity of brooms. === History === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251010 (3).jpg|thumb|Main sanctuary]] The date of the shrine’s founding is unknown. It is located in {{jalink|Iyo Province|Q907829}}, {{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}} (which differs somewhat from the modern district). The area is bordered by mountains and hills to the south and plains to the north, blessed with abundant water and apparently developed early; excavations have uncovered large and small {{jalink|kofun groups|Q11411019}}, large-scale {{jalink|ritual sites}}, and settlement remains. From ancient times, the shrine enjoyed deep reverence from the imperial family, and it is said that imperial envoys were dispatched during childbirths. The {{jalink|Iyo no Kuni Fudoki fragments}} record that {{jalink|Prince Shōtoku|Q263972}} toured Iyo District during a visit to Dōgo and worshiped at this shrine, dedicating a “divine name plaque.” In the Nara period, the shrine became a developing landholder, cultivating the surrounding area and forming an extensive sacred precinct of over eight chō, known as “Three Thousand Bō in Front and One Thousand Bō Behind.” Some shrine priests concurrently served as provincial officials, maintaining active exchanges with the central government, and the shrine became a cultural hub of Iyo District. In the Heian period, the shrine was listed in volumes 9 and 10 of the {{jalink|Engishiki|Q1342448}} (compiled in 927), which records shrines supported by the state, and it also appears in the {{jalink|Iyo Province Jinmyōchō}}, indicating its importance. It was granted the divine rank of Senior Third Rank and was also called “Takaoshiho Myōjin,” with frequent visits by imperial envoys and provincial governors. In the Kamakura period, it was known as “Takaoshihime Wakamiya Hachiman-gū” and attracted the devotion of the warrior class; in particular, {{jalink|Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800}} donated 76 chō of shrine land for the prosperity of the shogunate and for safe childbirth. Markets (three-times-a-month markets) were held at Ebisu shrines located to the northeast (kimon) and southwest (ura-kimon) of the shrine, drawing large crowds. The shrine families formed warrior bands centered on the Ōkannushi and Ōmiya-guji and served as shogunal {{jalink|gokenin|Q63765}}, while also being valued by the imperial court. During the Nanboku-chō period, they supported the Southern Court, and {{jalink|Emperor Go-Murakami|Q440447}} dispatched an imperial envoy who granted rank and confirmed shrine lands to the then head priest Tō no Hyōe. The local lord Kawano (Doi) Michikage also donated shrine lands and arms. In the Muromachi period, the markets at the Ebisu shrines expanded to six times a month and flourished. The shrine acted as the seat authority, protecting merchants and engaging in trade not only within Iyo Province but nationwide. However, in the Sengoku period, as warlords pursued policies of enriching the country and strengthening the military, the shrine gradually lost its lands, merchants and artisans were gathered into castle towns, and the markets rapidly declined.This market continued to be licensed until the Kanbun era (1661–1673) of the Edo period to meet local demand. In the Edo period, successive Matsuyama domain lords of the Hisamatsu family, including {{jalink|Katō Yoshiaki|Q1371657}} and {{jalink|Gamō Tadatomo|Q8513824}}, showed deep reverence for the shrine. As the domain’s prayer site, rites were performed for safe childbirth, protection from calamity, abundant harvests, rain, and clear weather, and it served as the guardian shrine of the Matsuyama domain and the general tutelary shrine of Iyo District. A domain lord’s proxy office used for official visits still survives, and until the end of the shogunate there was a room known as the “Lord’s Audience Chamber.” It is said that, because it is the only shrine in Japan to enshrine Takaoshihime Ōkami, it was bestowed the highest divine rank of Senior First Rank and the title “Only Shrine in the Nation” by the imperial court. The ox beam used in the tenshu of {{jalink|Matsuyama Castle|Q981357}}, raised in 1850, is said to have come from a great pine tree that stood at the northwest corner of the shrine precincts and was visible even from {{jalink|Tōon City|Q132412}}. An ancient well within the grounds never dries up even during droughts, and drinking its spring water is believed to bring fertility, making it an object of faith among women. At the birth of {{jalink|Princess Kiyomiya Takako}}, an imperial midwife requested prayers for a safe delivery, which were conducted by the shrine’s priests. === Main annual events === ; January 1 : New Year’s Day Festival ; January 3 : Daily Offering Association Opening Festival ; January 15 : Dondoyaki ritual ; February 3 : Setsubun Festival (Star Deity Festival) ; February 11 : Kinensai ; March 8 : Safe Childbirth and Good Fortune Grand Festival (Day of Mothers, Children, and Midwives) ; April 29 : Spring Grand Festival ; Second Sunday of May : Shōshikai ; August 2 : Mushiboshi Festival ; October 13–15 : Grand Annual Festival ; November 23 : Niiname-sai ; December 31 : Great Purification Rite and Year-End Festival === Shrine grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20250420 (2).jpg|thumb|Worship hall]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251012 (8).jpg|thumb|160px|Entwined branches]] ; Worship hall : Many rare votive tablets, including “Four Seasons of Agriculture” paintings, are displayed. ; Middle hall : Includes the “Domain Lord’s Proxy Office” and the offering preparation hall. ; Connecting hall (Narrow hall) ; Main sanctuary : A three-bay {{jalink|nagare-zukuri|Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof; periodic rebuilding was formerly carried out. ; Cloister ; Portable shrine storehouse ; Amulet office ; Purification pavilion ; Shrine gate ; Great torii === Sub-shrines within the grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (7).jpg|thumb|Ubusuna Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (1).jpg|thumb|Soga Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251010 (5).jpg|thumb|Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine]] ; Ubusuna Shrine : Affectionately called “Ubusuna-sama.” Taking home a round stone called the “Enman Stone” is believed to grant wishes; returning it in double quantity after the wish is fulfilled is said to bring even greater happiness. ; Soga Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Ōaza, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. Enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Nintoku|Q313119}}. ; Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}. Formerly located outside the grounds as the guardian of the shrine’s northeast demon gate. ; Kotohira Shrine : Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto and Emperor Sutoku. ; Tokumaru Tenman Shrine ; Ikume Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Emperor Ōjin|Q317997}} and {{jalink|Fujiwara no Kagekiyo|Q7676361}}. ; Amaterasu Kōtaijingū ; Narahara Shrine : Enshrines Uke Mochi-no-Kami. ; Shimoichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Hiruko-no-Mikoto||Ebisu-no-Kami}}; formerly guarded the shrine’s southwest demon gate. ; Ryūō Shrine : Formerly enshrined on an islet in a sacred pond; enshrines the Eight Great Dragon Kings. ; Funakawa Shrine (Funakawa Daimyōjin) ; Nanase Inari Shrine ; Suwa Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Kawara-gumi, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. ; Dōso Shrine ; Kasuga Shrine ; Tamahōki Shrine ; Ichinomiya Shrine : Enshrines {{jalink|Amemurakumo-no-Mikoto}}. ; Fukuga Shrine === Shrines outside the grounds === ; Soga Shrine : Located in Nakagawara Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Soga Shrine : Located in Ōma Ōaza; enshrines {{jalink|Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{jalink|Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto}}, and {{jalink|Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto}}. ; Hibuse Daimyōjin Shrine : Located within the Ōma Soga Shrine grounds; in the Edo period, magistrates stayed in seclusion there to pray for fire prevention and distributed talismans throughout the district. ; Narahara Shrine ; Sae Shrine ; Gotō Shrine (Gotō Daimyōjin) : Enshrines the ancestors of the Gotō family, a shrine family that maintained independence as jitō priests of Takaoshihime Shrine lands and served as the leading shrine priests of the Matsuyama domain. Until the late Edo period, they obtained official posts through the Hirohashi family and other court intermediaries without coming under the Yoshida family’s control. : The Gotō Shrine was enshrined near Miyashita in present-day {{jalink|Iyo City|Q838678}} until the mid-Edo period, but was relocated to its current site due to domain boundary issues. Image:Soga-jinja (Nakagawara, Masaki) 20241012 (1).jpg|Soga Shrine (Nakagawara) Image:Soga-jinja 20240630.jpg|Soga Shrine (Ōma) === On-site information === ; Address * {{jalink|Ehime Prefecture|Q123376}}{{jalink|Iyo District|Q1206427}}{{jalink|Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|Masaki Town}} Tokumaru 387 ; Access * Nearest station: {{jalink|Shikoku Railway Company|Q496531|JR}} {{jalink|Yosan Line|Q575016}} {{jalink|Kita-Iyo Station|Q898935}} (15 minutes on foot) === Notes === {{Reflist}} === References === * Masaki Town History Editorial Committee (ed.), *Masaki Town History*, 1979, Masaki Town Office * {{jalink|Nakarai Goan|Q121271640|Nakarai Goan}}, *Echoes of Ehime* (Iyo Historical Talks Society Series, Vol. 1), 1980, Iyo Historical Talks Society, Ehime Educational Books * Shikinaisha Research Association (ed.), *Shikinaisha Survey Report*, Vol. 23, 1987, Kōgakkan University Press * Shiga Tsuyoshi, *Research on Shikinaisha*, Vol. 1, 1977, Yūzankaku Publishing === External links === {{Commonscat|Takaoshihime-jinja}} * [http://www.takaoshihime.jp/ Official website of Takaoshihime Shrine] * [http://ehime-jinjacyo.jp/?p=5034 Takaoshihime Shrine (Ehime Prefecture Shrine Association)] * [https://www.facebook.com/pages/%E9%AB%98%E5%BF%8D%E6%97%A5%E8%B3%A3%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/556783461036023 Official Facebook page of Takaoshihime Shrine] {{Shinto sidebar}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Takaoshihime Shrine}} == Categories == [[Category:Wikidata generated shikinaisha pages|Takaoshihime Shrine]][[Category:Shikinaisha|Takaoshihime Shrine]][[Category:pages]][[Category:pages]] [[Category:Shinto shrine]] [[Category:Kokuhei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinaisha in Iyo Province]] [[Category:pages]] [[Category:愛媛県の神社]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の歴史]] [[Category:愛媛県松前町の建築物]] [[Category:伊予国の式内社]]

Torii gate |Location = {{ill|lt=Ehime Prefecture|Q123376|WD=Q123376}}{{ill|lt=Iyo District|Q1206427|WD=Q1206427}}{{ill|lt=Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|WD=Q1350953|lt=Masaki Town}} 387 Tokumaru |Coordinates = {{Wikicoord2dms|33|47|22.5|N|132|45|37|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = Takaoshihime Ōkami |Sacred object = |Shrine rank = {{ill||lt=Shikinaisha|UNKNOWN}}; former {{ill|lt=Gōsha|Q134917316|WD=Q134917316}} |Founded = Unknown |Main hall = Three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} |Other names = Takaoshi-sama, Takaoshiho Myōjin |Pilgrimage = |Annual festival= October 13, 14, and 15 each year |Rituals = Dondoyaki, Mushiboshi Festival, Midō pilgrimage |Map = {{Location map|Japan Ehime|label=Takaoshihime
Shrine|position=bottom|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=11|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=200}} }} '''Takaoshihime Shrine''' (たかおしひめじんじゃ, also written 高忍日売神社) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|lt=Ehime Prefecture|Q123376|WD=Q123376}}, {{ill|lt=Iyo District|Q1206427|WD=Q1206427}}, {{ill|lt=Masaki Town (Ehime Prefecture)|Q1350953|WD=Q1350953|lt=Masaki Town}}. Its name appears in the {{ill|lt=Engishiki Jinmyōchō|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}} under {{ill|lt=Iyo Province|Q907829|WD=Q907829}}, {{ill|lt=Iyo District|Q1206427|WD=Q1206427}}. It is the only shrine in Japan that enshrines Takaoshihime Ōkami and is revered as the ancestral deity of {{ill|lt=midwives|Q7832320|WD=Q7832320}} and {{ill|lt=wet nurses|Q472898|WD=Q472898}}, attracting particular devotion from {{ill|lt=midwives|Q185196|WD=Q185196}} nationwide as well as people involved in education. Within the shrine grounds stands the “Monument to Mothers, Children, and Midwives,” erected by the {{ill||lt=Muramatsu Shihoko Midwife Memorial Association|UNKNOWN}}, and an event is held there every year on March 8. The shrine crests used are the “Sixteen-petal Chrysanthemum” and the “5–7 Paulownia.” The annual festival is held every year on October 13, 14, and 15. === Enshrined deities === ; Principal deity : {{ill||lt=Takaoshihime Ōkami|UNKNOWN}} ; Associated deities : {{ill|lt=Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q97613650|WD=Q97613650}} : {{ill||lt=Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} : {{ill||lt=Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} === Mythology === Several unique myths have been handed down at this shrine. One concerns the birth of {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||WD=|lt=Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}, the father of the first emperor {{ill|lt=Emperor Jimmu|Q200188|WD=Q200188}}. When {{ill||lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} and {{ill||lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} were crossing the sea together in harmony, the goddess suddenly went into labor. They built a birthing hut on a nearby shore, and she gave birth there. They thatched the hut with ugaya (a term said by some to refer not to reeds but to a birthing hut itself)Some theories say this refers not to reeds but to a birthing hut., but many crabs crawled up from the sea and entered the hut, resulting in a very difficult labor. When {{ill||lt=Toyotama-hime no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} chanted the name of “Takaoshihime Ōkami” with single-minded devotion, Takaoshihime Ōkami appeared and dispatched {{ill||lt=Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill|lt=Ame no Oshio no Mikoto|Q462555|WD=Q462555|lt=Ame no Oshio no Mikoto}}. Ame no Oshihito no Mikoto and Ame no Oshio no Mikoto made brooms to sweep away the crabs, while Ame no Oshihime no Mikoto entered the hut and took on the role of midwife. As a result, although the birth was initially difficult, it became a safe delivery, and before the hut was fully completed, a healthy baby boy was born. {{ill||lt=Hikohohodemi no Mikoto|UNKNOWN}} named the boy {{ill|lt=Ugayafukiaezu no Mikoto||WD=|lt=Hikonagisa Takeugayafukiaezu no Mikoto}}. From this myth, the shrine’s deity has been revered by many people as the ancestral deity of midwives and wet nurses, and also as a deity of brooms. === History === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251010 (3).jpg|thumb|Main sanctuary]] The date of the shrine’s founding is unknown. It is located in {{ill|lt=Iyo Province|Q907829|WD=Q907829}}, {{ill|lt=Iyo District|Q1206427|WD=Q1206427}} (which differs somewhat from the modern district). The area is bordered by mountains and hills to the south and plains to the north, blessed with abundant water and apparently developed early; excavations have uncovered large and small {{ill|lt=kofun groups|Q11411019|WD=Q11411019}}, large-scale {{ill||lt=ritual sites|UNKNOWN}}, and settlement remains. From ancient times, the shrine enjoyed deep reverence from the imperial family, and it is said that imperial envoys were dispatched during childbirths. The {{ill||lt=Iyo no Kuni Fudoki fragments|UNKNOWN}} record that {{ill|lt=Prince Shōtoku|Q263972|WD=Q263972}} toured Iyo District during a visit to Dōgo and worshiped at this shrine, dedicating a “divine name plaque.” In the Nara period, the shrine became a developing landholder, cultivating the surrounding area and forming an extensive sacred precinct of over eight chō, known as “Three Thousand Bō in Front and One Thousand Bō Behind.” Some shrine priests concurrently served as provincial officials, maintaining active exchanges with the central government, and the shrine became a cultural hub of Iyo District. In the Heian period, the shrine was listed in volumes 9 and 10 of the {{ill|lt=Engishiki|Q1342448|WD=Q1342448}} (compiled in 927), which records shrines supported by the state, and it also appears in the {{ill||lt=Iyo Province Jinmyōchō|UNKNOWN}}, indicating its importance. It was granted the divine rank of Senior Third Rank and was also called “Takaoshiho Myōjin,” with frequent visits by imperial envoys and provincial governors. In the Kamakura period, it was known as “Takaoshihime Wakamiya Hachiman-gū” and attracted the devotion of the warrior class; in particular, {{ill|lt=Minamoto no Yoritomo|Q242800|WD=Q242800}} donated 76 chō of shrine land for the prosperity of the shogunate and for safe childbirth. Markets (three-times-a-month markets) were held at Ebisu shrines located to the northeast (kimon) and southwest (ura-kimon) of the shrine, drawing large crowds. The shrine families formed warrior bands centered on the Ōkannushi and Ōmiya-guji and served as shogunal {{ill|lt=gokenin|Q63765|WD=Q63765}}, while also being valued by the imperial court. During the Nanboku-chō period, they supported the Southern Court, and {{ill|lt=Emperor Go-Murakami|Q440447|WD=Q440447}} dispatched an imperial envoy who granted rank and confirmed shrine lands to the then head priest Tō no Hyōe. The local lord Kawano (Doi) Michikage also donated shrine lands and arms. In the Muromachi period, the markets at the Ebisu shrines expanded to six times a month and flourished. The shrine acted as the seat authority, protecting merchants and engaging in trade not only within Iyo Province but nationwide. However, in the Sengoku period, as warlords pursued policies of enriching the country and strengthening the military, the shrine gradually lost its lands, merchants and artisans were gathered into castle towns, and the markets rapidly declined.This market continued to be licensed until the Kanbun era (1661–1673) of the Edo period to meet local demand. In the Edo period, successive Matsuyama domain lords of the Hisamatsu family, including {{ill|lt=Katō Yoshiaki|Q1371657|WD=Q1371657}} and {{ill|lt=Gamō Tadatomo|Q8513824|WD=Q8513824}}, showed deep reverence for the shrine. As the domain’s prayer site, rites were performed for safe childbirth, protection from calamity, abundant harvests, rain, and clear weather, and it served as the guardian shrine of the Matsuyama domain and the general tutelary shrine of Iyo District. A domain lord’s proxy office used for official visits still survives, and until the end of the shogunate there was a room known as the “Lord’s Audience Chamber.” It is said that, because it is the only shrine in Japan to enshrine Takaoshihime Ōkami, it was bestowed the highest divine rank of Senior First Rank and the title “Only Shrine in the Nation” by the imperial court. The ox beam used in the tenshu of {{ill|lt=Matsuyama Castle|Q981357|WD=Q981357}}, raised in 1850, is said to have come from a great pine tree that stood at the northwest corner of the shrine precincts and was visible even from {{ill|lt=Tōon City|Q132412|WD=Q132412}}. An ancient well within the grounds never dries up even during droughts, and drinking its spring water is believed to bring fertility, making it an object of faith among women. At the birth of {{ill||lt=Princess Kiyomiya Takako|UNKNOWN}}, an imperial midwife requested prayers for a safe delivery, which were conducted by the shrine’s priests. === Main annual events === ; January 1 : New Year’s Day Festival ; January 3 : Daily Offering Association Opening Festival ; January 15 : Dondoyaki ritual ; February 3 : Setsubun Festival (Star Deity Festival) ; February 11 : Kinensai ; March 8 : Safe Childbirth and Good Fortune Grand Festival (Day of Mothers, Children, and Midwives) ; April 29 : Spring Grand Festival ; Second Sunday of May : Shōshikai ; August 2 : Mushiboshi Festival ; October 13–15 : Grand Annual Festival ; November 23 : Niiname-sai ; December 31 : Great Purification Rite and Year-End Festival === Shrine grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20250420 (2).jpg|thumb|Worship hall]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja 20251012 (8).jpg|thumb|160px|Entwined branches]] ; Worship hall : Many rare votive tablets, including “Four Seasons of Agriculture” paintings, are displayed. ; Middle hall : Includes the “Domain Lord’s Proxy Office” and the offering preparation hall. ; Connecting hall (Narrow hall) ; Main sanctuary : A three-bay {{ill|lt=nagare-zukuri|Q210684|WD=Q210684}} structure with a copper-sheeted roof; periodic rebuilding was formerly carried out. ; Cloister ; Portable shrine storehouse ; Amulet office ; Purification pavilion ; Shrine gate ; Great torii === Sub-shrines within the grounds === [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (7).jpg|thumb|Ubusuna Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251012 (1).jpg|thumb|Soga Shrine]] [[Image:Takaoshihime-jinja_20251010 (5).jpg|thumb|Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine]] ; Ubusuna Shrine : Affectionately called “Ubusuna-sama.” Taking home a round stone called the “Enman Stone” is believed to grant wishes; returning it in double quantity after the wish is fulfilled is said to bring even greater happiness. ; Soga Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Ōaza, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. Enshrines {{ill||lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill||lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}. ; Wakamiya Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{ill|lt=Emperor Nintoku|Q313119|WD=Q313119}}. ; Kamiichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{ill|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto||WD=|lt=Ebisu-no-Kami}}. Formerly located outside the grounds as the guardian of the shrine’s northeast demon gate. ; Kotohira Shrine : Enshrines Ōmononushi-no-Mikoto and Emperor Sutoku. ; Tokumaru Tenman Shrine ; Ikume Hachiman Shrine : Enshrines {{ill|lt=Emperor Ōjin|Q317997|WD=Q317997}} and {{ill|lt=Fujiwara no Kagekiyo|Q7676361|WD=Q7676361}}. ; Amaterasu Kōtaijingū ; Narahara Shrine : Enshrines Uke Mochi-no-Kami. ; Shimoichi Ebisu Shrine : Enshrines {{ill|lt=Hiruko-no-Mikoto||WD=|lt=Ebisu-no-Kami}}; formerly guarded the shrine’s southwest demon gate. ; Ryūō Shrine : Formerly enshrined on an islet in a sacred pond; enshrines the Eight Great Dragon Kings. ; Funakawa Shrine (Funakawa Daimyōjin) ; Nanase Inari Shrine ; Suwa Shrine : Formerly located in Tokumaru Kawara-gumi, moved into the grounds in the Meiji period. ; Dōso Shrine ; Kasuga Shrine ; Tamahōki Shrine ; Ichinomiya Shrine : Enshrines {{ill||lt=Amemurakumo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}. ; Fukuga Shrine === Shrines outside the grounds === ; Soga Shrine : Located in Nakagawara Ōaza; enshrines {{ill||lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill||lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}. ; Soga Shrine : Located in Ōma Ōaza; enshrines {{ill||lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, {{ill||lt=Kushinadahime-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}, and {{ill||lt=Ōkuninushi-no-Mikoto|UNKNOWN}}. ; Hibuse Daimyōjin Shrine : Located within the Ōma Soga Shrine grounds; in the Edo period, magistrates stayed in seclusion there to pray for fire prevention and distributed talismans throughout the district. ; Narahara Shrine ; Sae Shrine ; Gotō Shrine (Gotō Daimyōjin) : Enshrines the ancestors of the Gotō family, a shrine family that maintained independence as jitō priests of Takaoshihime Shrine lands and served as the leading shrine priests of the Matsuyama domain. Until the late Edo period, they obtained official posts through the Hirohashi family and other court intermediaries without coming under the Yoshida family’s control. : The Gotō Shrine was enshrined near Miyashita in present-day {{ill|lt=Iyo City|Q838678|WD=Q838678}} until the mid-Edo period, but was relocated to its current site due to domain boundary issues.
